name: Sign-in Failures (Brute Force/Bot Attacks)
hypothesis: CloudTrail logs contain failed console login attempts that could indicate brute force or bot attacks

name: Root Access Through Console
hypothesis: Root user console login attempts can be identified in CloudTrail logs

name: CloudTrail Disruption
hypothesis: Adversaries may attempt to disrupt CloudTrail logging to evade defenses

name: Unauthorized API Calls
hypothesis: Unauthorized API calls (AccessDenied or UnauthorizedOperation errors) can reveal application errors or malicious activity

name: Whoami Reconnaissance
hypothesis: Attackers use GetCallerIdentity API calls (similar to Unix 'whoami' command) to identify which AWS account they're using

name: Secrets Manager Access
hypothesis: Adversaries may attempt to retrieve secrets (certificates, credentials, sensitive data) from AWS Secrets Manager

name: Large EC2 Instance Creation
hypothesis: Attackers may attempt to launch extra-large EC2 instances (10xlarge or bigger) for cryptomining or other resource-intensive activities

name: S3 Bucket Brute Force
hypothesis: Attackers may brute force S3 bucket names by attempting GetBucketAcl requests

name: Suspicious User Agents
hypothesis: CloudTrail logs contain requests from suspicious user agents (containing 'kali', 'parrot', or 'powershell') indicating potential attacker tools

name: Suspicious User Agents
hypothesis: CloudTrail logs contain requests from suspicious user agents (containing 'command/*')

name: Permanent Key Creation
hypothesis: CreateAccessKey events from IAM users (not roles) can identify creation of long-term credentials
